title: St. John the Baptist (Monthly) — St. Abda the Bishop & St. Gennadius of Byzantium
narrative:
title": "St. John the Baptist (Monthly) — St. Abda the Bishop & St. Gennadius of Byzantium

narrative": "title\": \"St. John the Baptist (Monthly) — St. Abda the Bishop & St. Gennadius of Byzantium

narrative\": \"St. John the Baptist, the Forerunner and Prophet, St. Abda the Bishop-Martyr of Persia, and St. Gennadius the Patriarch of Constantinople

On the sixteenth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy, glorious, and ever-memorable Prophet and Forerunner John the Baptist, the last and greatest of the prophets, the one who leaped in his mother's womb at the approach of the Word still hidden in the womb of Mary, the one who was sent before the face of the Lord to prepare his ways, the one who stood in the Jordan and received into his hands the head of the eternal Son and pressed it beneath the waters of the river he had made his home. John is commemorated monthly in the Ethiopian calendar because he occupies the unique liturgical position of being simultaneously the last prophet and the first witness of the Incarnation: he closes the age of prophecy and opens the age of fulfilment, and his feast in each month is a reminder that the Church lives permanently in the space he defined — between the promise and its completion, between the voice crying in the wilderness and the Word who has come.

The theological depth of John's vocation, in the Ethiopian reading, lies in his absolute self-subordination to the one who comes after him: He must increase, but I must decrease. This sentence, which John speaks in the third chapter of the Gospel of John, is the tradition's most concentrated statement about the prophetic office in its fulfilled form. The prophet's function is not to be heard but to direct hearing elsewhere; not to be seen but to direct vision to the one who comes. John's decreasing is not a diminishment but a consummation: the forerunner who has fulfilled his function has become what he was always meant to be, a transparent pointing toward the one he proclaimed. The monthly feast of John in Ginbot is the Church's regular practice of this self-subordination: a community that returns monthly to the figure of decrease is a community being formed in the spirituality of self-giving.

Saint Abda the Bishop is a martyr of Persia whose story illuminates the situation of the Christian community in the Sasanian Empire during the fourth century: a bishop who, in the enthusiasm of the new Constantinian order — when Christianity had become the favoured religion of Rome and its bishop was building basilicas — made the mistake of destroying a Zoroastrian fire-temple. The Persian king Shapur II used this act as the pretext for a general persecution of Persian Christians that lasted forty years, and Abda himself was arrested and executed. The tradition does not celebrate Abda as a wise man — it acknowledges that his action was imprudent and politically catastrophic — but as a martyr, a man who paid with his life for an act of theological zeal that exceeded his diplomatic wisdom. His feast is the tradition's honest reflection on the complexity of martyrdom: not all who die for the faith die because they were wise, and the crown of martyrdom does not require that the recipient have made no mistakes on the way to receiving it.

Saint Gennadius of Constantinople is the great discovery of this day: Patriarch of Constantinople in the mid-fifth century, successor to Anatolius in the see of the New Rome, a man who governed his patriarchate during one of the most theologically turbulent periods in Byzantine history — the aftermath of the Council of Chalcedon — with a combination of doctrinal firmness and pastoral compassion that the tradition has found exemplary. He was known for a particular practice that has survived in the literature: he kept a list of the names of all the clergy of his diocese, and each morning before the Liturgy he would read the entire list aloud, praying for each person by name, so that the Eucharist he was about to celebrate was offered in the explicit consciousness of the whole community he served. This practice — the systematic, daily, named prayer for the entire people committed to one's care — is the tradition's most specific image of the episcopal vocation: the bishop who prays for all by name, who refuses to let any single member of the flock become anonymous, who insists on knowing and carrying every name. Amen.
